race.name: L'Etape Dubai by Tour de France
race.display_name: L'Etape Dubai by Tour de France
race.tagline: ASO's Tour de France franchise hits the Arabian desert -- 112 km of flat, fast highway riding through Dubai's skyline with classification jerseys and celebrity pros.

vitals.location: Dubai Design District (D3) to Expo Village Park, Dubai, UAE
vitals.registration: Online via dubai.letapeseries.com. Sprint pricing: EUR75 (early), EUR85 (standard), EUR95 (late). Includes TdF race experience and classification jersey competition.
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed zones on course with water, nutrition, and mechanical support
route_options: Epic Race: 112 km (70 mi), 257 m elevation gain -- the full race
Classic Race: 67 km (42 mi), 161 m elevation gain -- shorter option

climate.description: Late January is Dubai's coolest month and the optimal window for outdoor cycling. Daytime temperatures average 24-25C (75-77F), dropping to 14-15C (57-59F) at night. The 7 AM start means riders launch in cool conditions (15-18C) before temperatures climb through the morning. Humidity is moderate (54-63%). Rain is extremely rare -- roughly one day per month on average. The biggest climate factor is the desert sun, which is intense even in winter at this latitude.
challenges: Desert sun exposure: UV levels are high year-round, sunburn risk even in January
Morning fog possible -- can reduce visibility in early kilometers
Wind exposure on open desert highway sections
Temperature rises through the ride -- can reach 25C+ by late morning
Low humidity means dehydration sneaks up faster than expected

terrain.primary: Flat paved urban and desert highway
terrain.surface: 100% paved. The route uses 4-6 lane roads through Dubai, including the Dubai-Al Ain Road (E66). Roads are wide, smooth, and designed for high-speed traffic. The course begins with a 5 km loop around Dubai Design District with views of the Burj Khalifa and Downtown Dubai, then transitions to flat, fast highway riding through the desert to Expo Village Park. Essentially zero elevation change.
features: 5 km opening loop around D3 with Burj Khalifa skyline views
Wide 4-6 lane roads ideal for high-speed group riding
Flat and fast -- designed for power output and drafting, not climbing
Sprint sections for Green Jersey classification
KOM section for Polka Dot Jersey (minimal elevation involved)
Sagan Speed Challenge near Global Village -- timed sprint
Two distance options: Epic Race (112 km) and Classic Race (67 km)

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 50. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 51.

course_description.character: Flat, fast, and urban. The course opens with a scenic 5 km loop through Dubai Design District (D3) showcasing the Burj Khalifa and Downtown Dubai skyline, then transitions to wide, straight, 4-6 lane desert highway for the bulk of the distance, finishing at Expo Village Park. This is a power and aerodynamics course, not a climbing or technical course. The wide roads encourage group riding and drafting. Sprint sections for the Green Jersey and a nominal KOM section for the Polka Dot Jersey add classification interest.
course_description.signature_challenge: This is not a course with a signature climb or technical crux. The challenge is maintaining high power output on flat, exposed desert highway for 112 km. Wind is the variable -- a headwind on the return leg turns a fast ride into a grinding suffer-fest. Without group drafting skills, the exposed highway punishes solo riders ruthlessly.

logistics.airport: Dubai International Airport (DXB) -- one of the world's busiest airports, direct flights from virtually every major city globally. 20-30 minutes to Dubai Design District (D3) start area.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Business Bay, Downtown Dubai, or Dubai Design District for walking/cycling distance to the D3 start. Dubai has thousands of hotel rooms at every price point. January is peak tourist season -- book well in advance for better rates. Budget: $100-150/night (basic), $200-400/night (mid-range), $500+/night (luxury). Airbnb and apartment rentals available throughout the city.
logistics.food: Dubai's dining scene is world-class and extremely diverse -- every cuisine available. Carb-loading options are abundant. Post-race village at Expo Village Park includes food vendors and celebrations. Race feed zones stock water, energy drinks, and nutrition.

history.origin_story: L'Etape Dubai launched in February 2025 as part of ASO's global expansion of the L'Etape franchise -- bringing the Tour de France amateur experience to the Middle East for the first time. Dubai was announced as the 30th destination in the L'Etape Series worldwide. The inaugural edition on February 2, 2025 drew over 1,000 participants across a 101 km race and 50 km ride, starting from Dubai Design District (D3) and finishing at Expo Village Park. 18-year-old Syrian-UAE resident Ibrahiem Alrefai won the inaugural men's race in 2:17:24. The event attracted 243 international riders and 266 Emirati cyclists, making it the largest first-edition amateur cycling event in UAE history. The 2026 second edition expanded to 112 km Epic Race and 67 km Classic Race with new routes, attracted ~1,200 riders, and featured Tour de France legends Peter Sagan and Zdenek Stybar.
notable_moments: 2025 (Feb 2): Inaugural edition. 1,000+ riders. Ibrahiem Alrefai wins men's race (2:17:24). Olha Shekel wins women's (2:20:20). 101 km race / 50 km ride.

2026 (Jan 25): Second edition. ~1,200 riders. Majid Al Balooshi and Carolina Karlsson win Epic Race. Peter Sagan and Zdenek Stybar ride as celebrity ambassadors. Expanded to 112 km Epic / 67 km Classic.
2026: Sagan Speed Challenge sprint section added near Global Village for Green Jersey competition.
Dubai announced as 30th L'Etape Series destination globally.
history.reputation: The newest addition to ASO's global L'Etape franchise, bringing Tour de France branding to the Gulf region. Professionally organized with Dubai Sports Council backing and celebrity pro presence. The flat desert course limits its appeal compared to mountain-based L'Etape editions, but the event fills a gap in the Middle East cycling calendar and benefits from Dubai's extraordinary logistics infrastructure.

biased_opinion.summary: L'Etape Dubai brings the Tour de France name to the Middle East -- classification jerseys, Peter Sagan presenting trophies, Skoda lead cars, and the unmistakable yellow branding. The organization is professional, the Dubai Sports Council backing provides infrastructure support, and the January timing delivers pleasant desert winter conditions. But strip away the branding and you have 112 km of flat highway riding on 4-6 lane roads through a desert city. 257 meters of total elevation gain. The Polka Dot Jersey classification is almost theoretical. This is a well-organized group ride with Tour de France stickers, not a mountain battle.
strengths: Tour de France official franchise with ASO licensing -- legitimate TdF branding and classification jerseys
Dubai is a global travel hub with world-class infrastructure and January weather sweet spot
Celebrity pro appearances: Peter Sagan and Zdenek Stybar rode the 2026 edition
Reasonable entry fee (EUR75-95) for a TdF-branded event on fully closed roads
Professional organization by BE COOL / Peloton Events with Dubai Sports Council backing
Growing Middle East cycling market with genuine enthusiasm and government support
weaknesses: Essentially flat: 257m elevation over 112km is not a climbing event by any definition
Highway riding on 4-6 lane roads -- functional but not scenic or adventurous
Very new event (2nd edition in 2026) with no community history or tradition
Small field (1,000-1,200 riders) with limited competitive depth
Dubai trip costs are premium -- the entry fee is cheap, the trip is not
No meaningful technical or terrain challenge -- pure power output on flat tarmac
biased_opinion.bottom_line: L'Etape Dubai is a professionally organized franchise event that delivers the Tour de France atmosphere -- jerseys, pros, branding -- on a course that delivers almost nothing else. If you are based in the Gulf region, this is the most accessible way to experience TdF-branded racing without flying to Europe. If you are an international rider choosing among L'Etape franchise events, the original French edition, the Czech Republic edition, or Brazil offer dramatically better riding for the same brand experience. Dubai delivers logistics and weather. The road delivers watts per kilogram and a tailwind prayer.

final_verdict.one_liner: Tour de France branding, Peter Sagan, and classification jerseys on 112 km of dead-flat desert highway through Dubai.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you are based in the UAE or Gulf region and want a professionally organized TdF-branded event on your doorstep. Yes if you are visiting Dubai in January and want to combine tourism with a quality closed-road ride. Skip if you are choosing among L'Etape franchise events globally -- the French, Czech, or Brazilian editions offer vastly superior courses. Skip if you want climbing, technical terrain, or any challenge beyond holding watts on flat tarmac.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the real L'Etape experience with Alpine climbing: L'Etape du Tour de France (France, July). For a TdF franchise with actual mountains: L'Etape Czech Republic. For mass-participation road cycling in the Middle East with climbing: consider the Gran Fondo events linked to the UAE Tour.
